lens.included_activities: Independent and regional hardware retailers in the lower-middle-market band that repeatedly supply tools, builders' hardware, repair inputs, product advice, order preparation, pickup, and delivery to local households, tradespeople, and small commercial accounts.
lens.excluded_activities: Home centers classified elsewhere, manufacturer-owned or captive outlets, pure marketplace sellers without accountable fulfillment operations, passive product-holding entities, and businesses lacking a repeat external-customer relationship.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Predominantly merchandise margin on repeat consumer and professional purchases, supplemented in some stores by delivery, key cutting, rental, repair, account credit, and other services; prices are generally transactional rather than contracted cost-plus fees.
lens.deviation_from_default: The NAICS 2022 category combines legacy hardware stores with portions of electronic-shopping and direct-selling establishments. The lens is narrowed to operating retailers that provide repeat local fulfillment or advisory service, because pure nonstore product sellers have materially different labor and customer-retention economics.

a.bridge: BLS evidence covers retail salespersons and retail trade generally, not wage-weighted tasks in NAICS 444140. I map exposed checkout, product-search, routine customer-service, ordering, scheduling, marketing, bookkeeping, and inventory-analysis tasks onto a hardware retailer's wage bill, while treating shelf work, receiving, delivery, safety, and nuanced in-person advice as largely unexposed. Because hardware sales require unusually frequent physical presence and product-specific interaction, generic retail automation evidence likely overstates substitution for this lens.
a.rationale: Self-checkout and online search demonstrate that routine transaction and information tasks can be shifted to software or customers, but the occupation remains physically and socially intensive: BLS reports constant external verbal interaction for 73.8% of retail salespersons and an average 83.0% of the workday standing. The resulting opportunity is meaningful but clearly below a majority of current labor.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted task diary from 20-30 independent hardware stores, matched to payroll by role and separating already-automated POS work from remaining tasks, would materially tighten the range.
caveats: No published occupation-by-task study was found specifically for NAICS 444140.
The estimate excludes automation already embedded in POS, e-commerce, and replenishment systems.
The frozen compensation ratio is measured at ancestor NAICS 4441 and may not match independent hardware stores.

rho.rationale: Over five years, standard POS, forecasting, product-content, customer-message, workforce-scheduling, and bookkeeping tools should implement much of the exposed routine work. Realization is capped by fragmented item masters, small-store integration budgets, employee adoption, exception handling, and the need to supervise customer-facing AI and inventory decisions.
rho.change_evidence: Before-and-after labor-hour and error-rate data from Ace, Do it Best, or independent-store technology pilots using AI product search, replenishment, and service agents would materially change the implementation estimate.
caveats: This is bounded implementation judgment rather than a measured five-year adoption rate.
Small operators may lack clean product, inventory, and customer data.
Labor reductions may appear as avoided hiring or reassigned time rather than layoffs.

e.rationale: A substantial share of LMM hardware retailers should qualify because local stores serve repeat DIY and professional customers through accountable stocking, advice, pickup, and delivery. The exclusion is material, however, because the 2022 code also absorbs nonstore sellers and because some nominally independent outlets may be too transactional, captive, distressed, or owner-dependent to fit the operating lens.
e.change_evidence: A record-level Census Business Register or commercial establishment file segmented by ownership, channel, services, revenue, EBITDA, and repeat trade-account share would replace this judgment.
caveats: The frozen dataset provides no defensible n-band value, so this share cannot be paired with a reliable eligible-firm count.
NAICS classification is establishment-based while eligibility is firm-based.
The degree of repeat service is not observable in public business counts.

s5.bridge: Industry publications report owner-age and succession-plan survey results for independent home-improvement retailers and facility-level hardware transactions, not five-year firm-level control-transfer probability for eligible LMM firms. I use the 28 acquired deals in 2024 as evidence of an active transfer channel and the older-owner/limited-plan survey as evidence of a succession pipeline, then bound down for closures, greenfields, multi-store deals, family transfers, and stores outside the lens. Publication samples may overrepresent association members and visible deals, biasing the inferred rate upward.
s5.rationale: A Hardware Retailing reader survey found nearly 75% of respondents older than 46, while an earlier NRHA survey of more than 230 retailers found only 28% currently had succession plans. Separately, joint industry research counted 55 stores acquired in 28 deals during 2024. Together these support recurring transfer activity but not a majority probability over five years.
s5.change_evidence: Five years of PitchBook, GF Data, BizBuySell, cooperative membership-transfer records, and county-level ownership changes matched to eligible firms would provide a direct numerator and denominator.
caveats: The succession surveys are association/readership samples and one is dated 2016.
The deals report counts facilities and includes home centers, while the primitive concerns eligible firms and qualifying control transfers.
The missing n-band prevents calibration of reported deals to the target population.

q.bridge: Home Depot's filing describes competition and customer buying criteria for a much larger home-improvement retailer, not the discounted retained share of AI benefit at independent LMM hardware firms. I translate high price transparency and comparison shopping into substantial eventual pass-through, while local convenience, product availability, advice, delivery, and trade-account relationships allow partial retention. Scale retailers may face different procurement economics, so the direction of error is uncertain; stronger local differentiation would make this proxy understate retention, while weaker purchasing power would make it overstate retention.
q.rationale: Benefits first accrue through lower operating cost and better availability, but fixed merchandise pricing is readily compared and renewed purchase-by-purchase. Home Depot explicitly identifies price transparency, comparison shopping, aggressive promotion, customer experience, availability, and delivery as competitive dimensions. Roughly half of an implemented gross benefit is therefore expected to survive discounting and competitive response on a discounted five-year basis.
q.change_evidence: Store-level gross-margin, price-index, retention, and labor-productivity panels before and after comparable technology deployments, split between consumer and pro accounts, would directly test pass-through.
caveats: No source directly measures AI-benefit pass-through for independent hardware retailers.
Procurement cooperatives, local competition, and customer mix can create wide store-level variation.
The estimate excludes demand-volume effects, which belong in d5 and o.

d5.bridge: HIRI forecasts nominal product-market sales, not constant-price, constant-quality quantity purchased through eligible LMM retailers. I translate its 2.9% nominal hardware-market CAGR for 2026-2030 into low-single-digit real five-year quantity growth after allowing for product inflation, channel shifts, and the lens's local-store mix. Inflation and independent-store share loss could make the nominal forecast overstate lens demand.
d5.rationale: HIRI projects the total U.S. hardware product market to grow 2.9% annually from 2026 to 2030, with the consumer segment at about 2.8% and the professional segment at 2.6%. Maintenance, repair, and small projects support resilient quantity, but inflation, housing-cycle sensitivity, and online/big-box share pressure argue for only modest real growth in the current service basket.
d5.change_evidence: HIRI category unit-volume forecasts, Census Monthly Retail Trade data deflated by hardware-specific producer or consumer price indexes, and same-store unit data from independent cooperatives would materially narrow the range.
caveats: Forecasts cover the broader hardware/home-improvement product market rather than eligible firms.
Published growth is nominal; the required construct is constant-price and constant-quality quantity.
Five-year results remain sensitive to housing turnover, interest rates, tariffs, and weather.

o.bridge: BLS and Home Depot evidence describes retail work requirements and multichannel competition, not the share of future hardware demand requiring an accountable LMM operator. I treat online ordering and self-checkout as displacement of some storefront interaction, but not of procurement, inventory custody, fulfillment, returns, safety, credit, delivery, and exception responsibility. Evidence from a large chain may overstate the ease of omnichannel substitution for local independents.
o.rationale: Software can mediate search, recommendations, ordering, and checkout, yet hardware remains a physical product category requiring stocked inventory or coordinated fulfillment and an accountable seller. BLS also shows retail sales work remains interaction- and presence-heavy, while Home Depot describes customers moving between online research, stores, pickup, delivery, and post-purchase support. Most year-five quantity should still require a retailer-operator even if fewer tasks require people.
o.change_evidence: Customer journey data from independent-store cooperatives showing fully unattended online fulfillment, marketplace/direct-manufacturer substitution, return handling, and pro-account channel migration would directly test the operator-required share.
caveats: Operator-required does not mean every current store or job remains necessary.
Direct-to-consumer brands and mass e-commerce could displace more local-store volume than assumed.
The estimate concerns the lens's operator function, not exclusively a physical storefront.

narrative.executive_view: Independent hardware retail combines automatable transaction and information work with stubbornly physical fulfillment, local assortment, and customer advice. The practical opportunity is operational rather than transformational: better search, replenishment, service triage, scheduling, and back-office workflows can reduce avoidable labor, but the product and relationship still need an accountable operator.
narrative.ai_impact: AI can draft customer replies and promotions, normalize product content, improve search and recommendations, forecast replenishment, flag inventory anomalies, assist scheduling, and automate routine bookkeeping. It is less able to receive and shelve irregular goods, assess an in-person repair problem, safely recommend a fit-for-purpose item under ambiguity, cut keys, load orders, deliver products, or resolve physical exceptions.
narrative.value_capture: Retailers initially keep cost savings and availability gains, but transparent item prices and low-friction comparison make benefit pass-through likely over repeated purchases. Retention is strongest where the store wins on urgent availability, trusted advice, job-lot preparation, delivery, credit, or a dense trade-account relationship rather than on undifferentiated SKU price.
narrative.firm_availability: Aging owners, limited succession planning, and visible acquisition activity create a plausible control-transfer pipeline. Transferability is uneven because many stores depend on owner relationships, occupy owner-held real estate, or lack management depth, and the absence of a defensible LMM firm-count denominator is a major diligence gap.
narrative.demand_durability: Repair, maintenance, and small-project hardware demand should remain resilient, with published product-market forecasts pointing to modest nominal growth. Channel share can migrate online or to big-box and direct sellers, but the physical fulfillment, returns, and accountability function survives even when ordering becomes digital.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: The largest uncertainties are the 2022 code's inclusion of nonstore sellers, the missing target-firm denominator, reliance on broad retail occupation evidence, and the conversion of nominal product forecasts to real service quantity. A bad outcome would combine weak housing activity, aggressive big-box or online pricing, poor store data, key-person dependence, and an AI program that improves customer convenience without removing enough labor to repay integration costs.
narrative.principal_driver: Repeat local demand plus automatable transaction, product-information, inventory, and administrative workflows.
narrative.principal_weakness: Price-transparent merchandise retail limits retained savings, while the eligible LMM firm count is missing and many tasks remain physical.

sources.title: The North American Industry Classification System in the Current Employment Statistics Program
sources.supports: NAICS 2022 hardware retailers combine legacy hardware stores with portions of electronic-shopping and direct-selling establishments, supporting the heterogeneous-channel lens.

sources.title: Retail salespersons: Occupational Requirements Survey factsheet
sources.supports: In 2025, external verbal interaction was constant for 73.8% of retail salespersons; workers averaged 83.0% of the day standing, evidencing social and physical constraints on substitution.

sources.title: Industry and occupational employment projections overview and highlights, 2024-34
sources.supports: BLS attributes projected cashier losses to self-checkout and notes online shopping pressure on retail salespersons, while e-commerce supports logistics jobs.

sources.title: How 2 Families Have Passed Operations to the Next Generation
sources.supports: A Hardware Retailing reader survey found nearly 75% of respondents were older than 46, indicating succession relevance.

sources.title: Putting the Success in Succession Planning
sources.supports: NRHA surveyed more than 230 retailers: 41% had participated in succession planning, 28% had a current plan, and 27% did not know where to start.

sources.title: The Home Depot, Inc. fiscal 2024 Form 10-K
sources.supports: Describes a highly competitive, fragmented, multichannel home-improvement market; internet-driven price transparency; and competition on experience, price, availability, delivery, and pro support.

sources.title: 2024 Retail Hardware Deals Report
sources.supports: Joint research recorded 55 hardware-store facilities acquired in 28 deals during 2024, alongside 81 openings and 39 closures.

sources.title: Hardware Market Watch: Trends in Locks, Fasteners, Gate and Decorative Hardware in 2026
sources.supports: HIRI reports a $19.876 billion consumer and $2.538 billion professional hardware market in 2025 and forecasts 2.9% annual total growth for 2026-2030.

sources.title: 2026 Market Measure
sources.supports: NHPA reports 2.4% expected 2026 sales growth and cites HIRI's 3.8% total-market projection, with DIY and emergency-driven demand remaining strong amid housing and tariff uncertainty.
